title: Advanced Features
text: splash screen occupies window backend starts, replacing blank screen useful feedback. WebAssembly loading: Shinylive apps can take 5-10 seconds initialize Branding: Display logo app name wait User feedback: Shows app starting, frozen Add _shinyelectron.yml: Create splash image (recommended: PNG, 300x200px minimum) Place app directory Reference configuration: Tip best results, use transparent PNG solid background color.
code: splash:   enabled: true   duration: 3000          # Minimum display time (ms)   background: "#ffffff"   # Background color   image: "splash.png"     # Path relative to app directory   width: 400   height: 300   text: "Loading..."   text_color: "#333333" splash:   enabled: true   image: "assets/splash-logo.png"   background: "#1a1a2e"   text: "Starting Dashboard..."   text_color: "#eaeaea"

text: System tray (menubar macOS) allows app run background provides quick access via icon. Background services: Apps keep running closed Quick access: Open app tray double-click Status indicators: Show app state tray menu default tray menu includes: Show: Brings window front Quit: Exits application Note macOS, use template images (black icons transparency) proper appearance light dark modes.

text: can also configure features programmatically:
code: # Read current config config <- read_config("path/to/app")  # Enable splash screen config$splash$enabled <- TRUE config$splash$duration <- 3000 config$splash$text <- "Loading Dashboard..."  # Enable system tray config$tray$enabled <- TRUE config$tray$close_to_tray <- TRUE  # Write updated config yaml::write_yaml(config, file.path("path/to/app", "_shinyelectron.yml"))

title: Automatic Updates
text: shinyelectron uses electron-updater provide automatic updates. electron-updater library electron-builder uses check , download, install updates. Updates can distributed via:

title: Automatic Updates
text: Use enable_auto_updates() configure app: modifies _shinyelectron.yml configuration file. Verify configuration:
code: # Enable GitHub-based updates enable_auto_updates(   "path/to/app",   provider = "github",   owner = "your-username",   repo = "your-app-repo" ) check_auto_update_status("path/to/app")

title: Automatic Updates

title: Automatic Updates
text: Verify configuration:
code: check_auto_update_status("path/to/app")

title: Automatic Updates
text: GitHub Releases recommended approach open-source projects. Build application normally: Navigate repository GitHub Click “Releases” → “Create new release” Create tag (e.g., v1.0.0) MyApp-1.0.0.dmg (macOS) MyApp-Setup-1.0.0.exe (Windows) MyApp-1.0.0.AppImage (Linux) Publish release Note file names must match pattern {productName}-{version}.{ext} electron-updater detect correctly. publish new release higher version number, app : Check updates startup (check_on_startup = TRUE) Notify user update available Download update (automatically auto_download = TRUE) Prompt restart install
code: enable_auto_updates(   "path/to/app",   provider = "github",   owner = "myorg",   repo = "my-shiny-dashboard",   check_on_startup = TRUE,   auto_download = FALSE   # Prompt user before downloading ) export("path/to/app", destdir = "build")

title: Automatic Updates
text: _shinyelectron.yml: three settings control update pipeline: check_on_startup asks “update?”, auto_download fetches , auto_install applies . Recommended apps: critical updates:
code: updates:   enabled: true   provider: "github"   check_on_startup: true    # Check when app starts   auto_download: false      # Download without prompting   auto_install: false       # Install on app quit    github:     owner: "your-username"     repo: "your-repo"     private: false          # Set true for private repos updates:   check_on_startup: true   auto_download: false    # Let user decide   auto_install: false     # Let user decide when to restart updates:   check_on_startup: true   auto_download: true     # Download immediately   auto_install: true      # Install on next quit

title: Configuration Guide
text: complete configuration file available options:
code: # shinyelectron configuration file # Documentation: https://r-pkg.thecoatlessprofessor.com/shinyelectron/  app:   name: "My Application"     # Application display name   version: "1.0.0"           # Application version (used in package metadata)  build:   type: "r-shinylive"        # Application type   runtime_strategy: "auto-download"  # For native types: bundled, system, auto-download, container   platforms:                 # Target platforms     - mac     - win     - linux   architectures:             # Target architectures     - x64     - arm64  r:   version: null              # R version for bundled/auto-download (null = latest release)  python:   version: null              # Python version for bundled/auto-download (null = "3.12.10")  window:   width: 1200                # Default window width (pixels)   height: 800                # Default window height (pixels)  server:   port: 3838                 # Development server port  icons:                       # Platform-specific icons   mac: "icons/icon.icns"   win: "icons/icon.ico"   linux: "icons/icon.png"  nodejs:   version: null              # Node.js version (null = latest LTS)   auto_install: false        # Auto-install Node.js if not found  container:                   # Used when runtime_strategy is "container"   engine: "docker"           # "docker" or "podman"   image: null                # Container image (null = auto-select)   tag: "latest"   pull_on_start: true        # Pull latest image on app start   volumes: []                # Additional volume mounts   env: []                    # Additional environment variables  # Multi-app suite (2+ apps packaged in one Electron shell) # apps: #   - id: "dashboard" #     name: "Dashboard" #     path: "./apps/dashboard" #     type: "r-shiny"          # Optional per-app override (default: build.type) #     description: "Main dashboard" #     icon: "icons/dash.png" #   - id: "admin" #     name: "Admin Panel" #     path: "./apps/admin"

title: Container Strategy
text: container strategy good fit : app complex system dependencies (C libraries, database drivers, spatial tools like GDAL/PROJ — geospatial libraries notoriously difficult install) hard bundle portably. Reproducibility critical – container image pins every dependency OS layer. team already uses Docker workflows deployment want desktop app match server environment. distributing known set users (e.g., within organization) can require Docker Podman. apps without system-level dependencies, auto-download bundled strategies simpler require end users install Docker.

title: Container Strategy
text: end user’s machine must one : Docker Desktop – https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/ Podman – https://podman.io/getting-started/installation engine must running Electron app launches. shinyelectron detects available engines automatically, checking Docker first Podman. build machine, Docker/Podman optional. present, shinyelectron validates daemon reachable; , warns proceeds – image built pulled end user’s machine first launch.

title: Container Strategy
text: Think container sealed box: app, runtime, system dependencies live inside, completely isolated user’s machine. Electron just window shows ’s inside box. startup sequence user opens packaged Electron app: Electron launches shows lifecycle splash screen. container.js backend resolves Docker socket – checking docker context inspect, well-known paths (/var/run/docker.sock, ~/.colima/docker.sock, named pipes Windows). detects engine (Docker Podman) config availability. image present locally, built embedded Dockerfile pulled registry. container starts docker run -d, mapping host port bind-mounting app directory /app. Bind-mounting maps directory host container, like creating shared folder two machines. backend polls Shiny server responds (120 seconds). Electron loads http://localhost:<port>. quit, container stopped removed.

title: Container Strategy
text: Set runtime_strategy: container _shinyelectron.yml: Setting image: null (default) tells shinyelectron embed Dockerfile Electron package build image locally first launch. Setting image registry reference (e.g., ghcr.io/myorg/myapp) pulls registry instead.
code: app:   name: "My Containerized App"   version: "1.0.0"  build:   type: "r-shiny"   runtime_strategy: "container"  container:   engine: "docker"         # "docker" or "podman"   image: null              # null = use embedded Dockerfile   tag: "latest"   pull_on_start: true   volumes: {}              # extra host:container volume mounts   env: {}                  # extra environment variables  server:   port: 3838

title: Container Strategy
text: shinyelectron ships built-Dockerfile R apps based rocker/r2u:24.04, provides pre-compiled binary R packages via apt. supports amd64 arm64 architectures. embedded Dockerfile installs r-cran-shiny r-cran-jsonlite. App dependencies dependencies.json installed container startup entrypoint script, launches:
code: export(   appdir = "path/to/my-r-app",   destdir = "path/to/output",   app_name = "My R App",   app_type = "r-shiny",   runtime_strategy = "container" ) Rscript --vanilla -e "shiny::runApp('/app', port = 3838, host = '0.0.0.0')"

title: Container Strategy
text: Python apps, embedded Dockerfile uses python:3.12-slim pre-installs shiny via pip. entrypoint installs additional packages dependencies.json, starts app python3 -m shiny run --port 3838 --host 0.0.0.0 --app-dir /app.

title: Container Strategy
text: apps specialized system dependencies, place Dockerfile app directory. shinyelectron embed use build image first launch. Dockerfile must: Expose port specified PORT environment variable (default 3838). Set working directory /app (app files bind-mounted runtime). Accept PORT HOST environment variables server bind address. Example R app needs spatial libraries: Example Python app machine learning dependencies:
code: FROM rocker/r2u:24.04 RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \     r-cran-shiny r-cran-sf r-cran-terra libgdal-dev libproj-dev \     && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* WORKDIR /app EXPOSE 3838 CMD ["Rscript", "--vanilla", "-e", \      "shiny::runApp('/app', port=as.integer(Sys.getenv('PORT',3838)), host=Sys.getenv('HOST','0.0.0.0'), launch.browser=FALSE)"] FROM python:3.12-slim RUN pip install --no-cache-dir shiny pandas scikit-learn WORKDIR /app EXPOSE 3838 CMD ["python3", "-m", "shiny", "run", "--port", "3838", \      "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--app-dir", "/app", "--no-dev-mode"]

title: Container Strategy
text: container-specific security considerations (volume mounts, root access, container escapes), see Security Considerations. End user requirement. Unlike bundled auto-download, container strategy requires end user Docker Podman installed running. makes unsuitable broad public distribution control user’s environment. First-launch startup time. first time app runs, container image must built (embedded Dockerfile) pulled (registry). can take several minutes depending image size network speed. Subsequent launches use cached image start seconds. Nested virtualization. Running Docker inside virtual machine (e.g., Parallels Desktop VMware macOS) may fail due nested virtualization limitations. Users situation use Podman, require hypervisor Linux, run host OS directly. Container daemon must running. Docker Desktop started, app display error directing user start . way Electron app start daemon automatically.

title: Container Strategy
text: Docker Desktop licensing. Docker Desktop requires paid subscription commercial use larger organizations. Podman free, open-source, daemonless alternative shinyelectron supports drop-replacement — set engine: "podman" config, let shinyelectron auto-detect . Architecture matching. shinyelectron passes --platform linux/arm64 --platform linux/amd64 Docker based host’s CPU architecture. building buildx, uses --load ensure image available locally. Apple Silicon Macs, avoids performance penalty running amd64 images Rosetta emulation. Colima users (macOS). use Colima instead Docker Desktop, shinyelectron checks ~/.colima/docker.sock automatically. extra configuration needed.

title: Getting Started with shinyelectron
text: default path converts Shiny app run entirely inside browser via WebR (R) Pyodide (Python). WebR compiles R WebAssembly runs directly browser. Pyodide Python. end user needs nothing installed — everything self-contained. R Python export(   appdir = "path//-app",       # directory containing app.R   destdir = "-electron-app",   app_name = "App" ) R Python packages don’t work WebAssembly. full compatibility, use native path — app runs real R Python process instead browser.  Three execution modes: Shinylive runs entirely -browser, Native spawns real R Python process, Container runs inside Docker Podman.
code: export(   appdir = "path/to/my-app",       # directory containing app.R   destdir = "my-electron-app",   app_name = "My App" ) export(   appdir = "path/to/my-py-app",    # directory containing app.py   destdir = "my-py-electron-app",   app_name = "My App",   app_type = "py-shinylive" ) # R Shiny with auto-download (default: downloads R on first launch) export(   appdir = "path/to/my-app",   destdir = "my-native-app",   app_type = "r-shiny" )  # Python Shiny with bundled runtime (ships Python inside the app) export(   appdir = "path/to/my-py-app",   destdir = "my-bundled-py-app",   app_type = "py-shiny",   runtime_strategy = "bundled" )

title: Multi-App Suites
text: Electron app starts, users see launcher page instead loading app immediately. launcher displays card app suite, showing name, description, type badge, icon (either custom image generated initial). Clicking card starts backend app navigates . using app, Apps menu menu bar provides “Back Launcher” option stops current backend returns launcher page. apps suite share single runtime process (one R Python instance). user switches one app another: current backend process stopped. launcher page shown. user selects next app. new backend process starts selected app. means one app runs time, keeping resource usage low. need install multiple runtimes manage parallel processes.

text: Shinylive use runtime strategy. choose app_type = "r-shinylive" app_type = "py-shinylive", Shiny app runs entirely browser using WebR Pyodide. server-side R Python process — everything executes client-side inside Electron window. shinylive types run browser, runtime_strategy parameter ignored. set : export() calls shinylive R package (R) shinylive CLI (Python) convert app static assets. Electron app serves assets via local Express server required COOP/COEP headers. browser security headers enable shared memory WebR needs run efficiently. WebR Pyodide boots inside browser runs app code. Zero runtime dependencies end user. native process manage clean . Works platforms (Windows, macOS, Linux). Large app size due WebR/Pyodide assets (~50–100 MB R). R packages work WebR. Packages compiled C/Fortran code need WebR-compatible WASM builds. Python packages must available Pyodide. Slower initial page load WASM runtime boots. Choose shinylive app uses packages available WebR/Pyodide want simplest deployment runtime management.

Electron shell communicates containerized app local port. can also configure container engine image _shinyelectron.yml: export() packages app includes container.js backend module. launch, Electron detects Docker Podman system. pulls specified image (pull_on_start true) starts container app directory mounted. title: Security Considerations
text: Electron app two process types: Main process: Runs Node.js full OS access. creates windows, manages app lifecycle, spawns backend servers (R, Python, containers). Renderer process: Displays web content (Shiny app). default sandboxed isolated Node.js APIs. key security principle : never give renderer process direct access Node.js. renderer nodeIntegration enabled, JavaScript running Shiny app – including injected scripts XSS vulnerabilities – can execute arbitrary OS commands. full list Electron security recommendations, see Electron Security Checklist.

title: Security Considerations
text: shinyelectron generates Electron configuration secure defaults box: setting : Preload script uses contextBridge. preload script acts secure intermediary — renderer can call predefined functions access Node.js directly. exposes narrow IPC interface (lifecycle.onStatus, lifecycle.retry, lifecycle.quit, etc.) via contextBridge.exposeInMainWorld(). renderer never gets direct access ipcRenderer Node.js module. Dev tools default. Electron DevTools menu item included show_dev_tools enabled configuration. production builds, leave disabled.
code: // From the generated main.js BrowserWindow config webPreferences: {   nodeIntegration: false,   contextIsolation: true,   sandbox: true,   enableRemoteModule: false,   webSecurity: true,   preload: path.join(__dirname, 'preload.js'),   partition: 'persist:<app_slug>' }

title: Security Considerations
text: container strategy (runtime_strategy: "container") runs Shiny app inside Docker Podman container. provides meaningful isolation: app access host filesystem unless explicitly mount volumes via container.volumes _shinyelectron.yml. Network access limited published port. container runs non-root user (default shinyelectron images). However, containers full security boundary: Docker requires root-equivalent access Linux (Docker daemon runs root). Podman runs rootless default. Mounted volumes give container read/write access host directories. mount app needs. Container escape vulnerabilities, rare, exist. Keep Docker/Podman updated.

title: Security Considerations
text: common Electron security mistakes. shinyelectron’s defaults prevent , can undo modifying generated Electron files. modify settings generated Electron code set nodeIntegration: true webPreferences. gives every script renderer full Node.js access, including injected via XSS. set webSecurity: false. disables -origin policy, allowing page make requests origin. set contextIsolation: false. lets page scripts access preload script’s scope, breaking security boundary. load remote URLs main window. shinyelectron loads localhost (native backends) local files (lifecycle pages). Loading external URL means running untrusted code app’s Electron privileges. ship debug builds. Disable show_dev_tools production. DevTools let users (malware) inspect modify running app, execute arbitrary JavaScript, access Node.js console main process.

title: Troubleshooting Guide
text: Symptom: packaged app find Rscript end user’s machine using system runtime strategy. Cause: default R installer Windows places R C:\Program Files (C:\Program Files (x86) 32-bit), add system PATH. Solutions: R installation: Check option add R PATH. installation: Add R bin directory manually. example: production: Use bundled auto-download runtime strategy end users need R pre-installed. Symptom: Extracting runtime archive fails : Cause: Git Windows ships GNU tar PATH. GNU tar interprets paths like C:\... remote host specification (host:path). runtime downloader needs Windows 10’s built-bsdtar (%SystemRoot%\System32\tar.exe), handles drive letters correctly. shinyelectron works around automatically: JavaScript runtime downloader resolves full path System32\tar.exe, R-side extraction uses R’s internal tar. still hit error, check whether something overridden tar command: first result C:\Windows\System32\tar.exe, C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\tar.exe. Symptom: Cause: Windows, running Electron process locks executable DLLs. try rebuild app previous build still running, npm / electron-builder overwrite locked files. Solution: Close running Electron app. process lingers, end Task Manager (electron.exe). Re-run build. Symptom: Builds extremely slow, especially npm install step final packaging step. Cause: Windows Defender real-time scanning inspects every file npm creates inside node_modules (thousands small files). Solutions: Add exclusion project directory: Settings > Privacy & Security > Virus & threat protection > Manage settings > Exclusions > Add exclusion > Folder Add exclusion shinyelectron cache directory (~/.shinyelectron).

title: Troubleshooting Guide
text: Symptom: Users see “app damaged can’t opened” “app can’t opened Apple check malicious software” launching packaged app. Cause: macOS GateKeeper blocks unsigned unnotarized applications. Solutions: distribution: Sign notarize app. Configure signing _shinyelectron.yml: Set environment variables APPLE_TEAM_ID, APPLE_ID, APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD building. local testing : Right-click app choose “Open”, confirm. bypasses GateKeeper specific app. remove quarantine attribute: Symptom: Windows SmartScreen shows “Windows protected PC” user tries run installer app. Cause: executable unsigned enough SmartScreen reputation. Solutions: distribution: Sign app code signing certificate. Configure _shinyelectron.yml: Set CSC_LINK CSC_KEY_PASSWORD environment variables building. local testing: Click “info” “Run anyway”. EV certificates receive immediate SmartScreen reputation. Standard certificates build reputation time users run signed installer.
code: signing:   sign: true   mac:     identity: "Developer ID Application: Your Name (TEAMID)"     team_id: "TEAMID"     notarize: true xattr -cr /Applications/MyApp.app signing:   sign: true   win:     certificate_file: "path/to/cert.pfx"

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Added Python Shiny support: py-shinylive (browser-based via Pyodide) py-shiny (native Python runtime). four app types now fully implemented.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Added four runtime strategies native apps (r-shiny, py-shiny): system, bundled, auto-download (default), container. Bundled strategy embeds portable R Python runtime inside Electron app zero-dependency distribution. Auto-download strategy downloads runtime first launch caches locally. Container strategy runs apps inside Docker Podman full environment isolation.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Bundle multiple Shiny apps single Electron shell launcher UI. Configure via _shinyelectron.yml apps array. Python suites read dependencies single suite-root requirements.txt.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: New lifecycle splash screen progressive status updates startup. Backend stderr parsed surfaced splash (package loading, font downloads, server status) users see ’s happening instead frozen screen. Port allocation uses OS-assigned ports via findAvailablePort() prevent collisions multiple apps run simultaneously.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Added sign parameter export() build_electron_app(). Supports macOS code signing + notarization Windows Authenticode via environment variables (CSC_LINK, CSC_KEY_PASSWORD, APPLE_ID, APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD).

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: app_check(): pre-flight validator reports issues without aborting. wizard(): interactive configuration generator _shinyelectron.yml. available_examples() example_app(): browse retrieve bundled demo apps. sitrep_shinyelectron() now checks Python shinylive CLI shiny package availability.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Fixed fs::dir_copy() creating nested subdirectories Windows introducing copy_dir_contents() consistent cross-platform semantics. Fixed GNU tar (Git Windows) misinterpreting drive letters remote hosts resolving %SystemRoot%\System32\tar.exe (bsdtar) explicitly. Fixed Python download URL: python-build-standalone ships .tar.gz platforms, .zip Windows. Fixed bundled R package installation: packages now installed portable R’s library scrubbed environment (--vanilla, R_LIBS_USER=""). Added post-copy entrypoint validation catch file layout issues build time instead inside running Electron app.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: New vignettes: Runtime Strategies, Multi-App Suites, Code Signing, Container Strategy, Security Considerations. Updated Getting Started, Configuration, Troubleshooting vignettes Python support, runtime strategies, Windows-specific guidance.

title: shinyelectron 0.2.0
text: Extracted run_command_safe() helper repeated processx::run() patterns. Extracted killProcessTree() shared JS utils process cleanup. Added app type group constants (SHINYLIVE_TYPES, NATIVE_TYPES, R_TYPES, PY_TYPES) reduce stringly-typed code. Cached available.packages() bundled R builds avoid redundant CRAN network calls. Fixed event listener leak multi-app mode (removeAllListeners() instead removeAllListeners('status')).

title: shinyelectron 0.1.0
text: Initial release r-shinylive support. Export R Shiny apps standalone Electron desktop applications via WebR. Cross-platform builds macOS, Windows, Linux. Node.js local installation management. Configuration via _shinyelectron.yml. Automatic updates via electron-updater.
